title: Redesigning I Ching symbols
content_html:
Redesigning I Ching symbols

I-ching is composed of hexagrams which are straight lines. How might these symbols be represented differently, for example, as curves or interlocking shapes? I want to create a different graphical representation whilst retaining the logic and structure of the hexagram system of symbols

That’s a brilliant idea — to reinterpret the I Ching’s hexagrams through curves, interlocking shapes, or organic geometries while preserving their internal logic (yin/yang, binary layering, and relational symmetry).

Let’s unpack this carefully so that you can design a visual system that is both faithful and newly expressive.

⸻

1. The Original Logic of the Hexagram

Each hexagram is a stack of six lines, each either:

•	**Yang (solid line — “⚊”)**
•	**Yin (broken line — “⚋”)**

Thus: • Each hexagram encodes a 6-bit binary pattern (2⁶ = 64 possibilities). • The lower trigram (3 lines) and upper trigram (3 lines) can each be read independently, so each hexagram = (trigram × trigram). • Transitions between hexagrams (via line change) express change and transformation.

This binary and hierarchical logic should remain intact in any new visual system.

⸻

2. Re-imagining the Line as a Curve or Shape

Here are several visual translation strategies:

Traditional Element Conceptual Essence Possible Curved / Interlocking Equivalent Yang line (⚊) Unbroken, active, outward flow Continuous curve, convex arc, rising wave, closed ring, or bold stroke Yin line (⚋) Divided, receptive, inward flow Interrupted curve, concave arc, open circle, gap, dashed stroke, or two mirrored curves Trigram (3 lines) Compound of forces (Heaven, Earth, etc.) Nested trio of arcs, three lobes of a petal, tri-part spiral, three-fold knot Hexagram (6 lines) Interaction of two trigrams (macro-micro) Two interlocking motifs — upper and lower forms joined at a seam or mirrored axis

⸻

3. Structural Translation Framework

Think of a mapping function from line position → geometric parameter:

Line Position (bottom = 1) Role Visual Variable 1–2 (Lower Trigram) Foundation / Earth Radius, curvature, thickness 3–4 (Middle) Human / Mediation Phase or orientation 5–6 (Upper Trigram) Heaven / Potential Opacity, colour hue, or outward expansion

Thus each hexagram becomes a parametric glyph, not just stacked bars.

For instance:

•	Yang = curvature = +1, Yin = curvature = –1
•	Map line index to radius → generate 6 concentric arcs
•	Alternate filled and open segments → organic mandala

⸻

4. Possible Design Families

A. Waveform Hexagrams

Each line → sinusoidal curve:

•	Yang = full amplitude
•	Yin = half amplitude, or phase-shifted

Stack vertically or wrap around a circle to create continuous wave bands. → Visual resemblance to sound / energy fields.

B. Circular or Radial Hexagrams

Unfold the six lines around a circle:

•	Yin/Yang become alternating **arcs** (present/absent)
•	Lower trigram = inner ring; upper trigram = outer ring

→ Each hexagram = unique two-ring pattern, like a fingerprint.

C. Interlocking Petals / Knots

Represent Yin as open, Yang as closed, then use six linked petals or loops.

→ Produces organic, mandala-like geometry reminiscent of Celtic knots or DNA helices.

D. Vector-Field Interpretation

Treat each line as a vector or flow direction:

•	Yang = outward pointing arrow
•	Yin = inward curve

Use a circular field where each line defines a vector angle → the full hexagram = a field configuration.

⸻

5. Maintaining Logical Mappings

You could encode values numerically (binary → 0/1 → curve parameter). Example:

0 (yin) = curve bends left
1 (yang) = curve bends right

For hexagram ䷀ (Qian = 111111): all curves bend right → perfect symmetry.

For hexagram ䷁ (Kun = 000000): all curves bend left → opposite polarity.

Intermediate hexagrams show alternating curvature.

This preserves the computational logic while changing the visual vocabulary.

⸻

6. Dynamic or Transformational Representation

Because I Ching is about change, animate transitions:

•	Lines **morph** from straight to curved.
•	Yin gaps **close** and **open** dynamically.
•	Two hexagrams’ difference → “delta pattern” drawn as **interference between curves**.

⸻

7. Inspirations & Analogues

Inspiration Source Relevance Yijing calligraphy Already emphasizes brush-curve energy in the line; use this as stylistic base. Topological diagrams Curved continuity while maintaining discrete structure. Yin–Yang taijitu Circular logic of duality mapped to hexagram pairs. Genetic code helix diagrams Parallel six-bit binary to codon triplets. Neural network or circuit schematics Shows connections between layers (trigrams).

⸻

8. Example Concept

“Curved Hexagram Mandala” • 6 concentric circles. • Each circle divided into 2 segments (Yin = gap; Yang = filled). • Lower trigram = inner 3 circles, upper = outer 3. • Use colours or curvature to express element (Heaven, Earth, Water, Fire…).

Each hexagram becomes a unique radial signature, readable but visually organic.

⸻

9. Implementation Notes

You can prototype this in Processing or p5.js:

•	Input: six-bit binary array [1,0,1,1,0,0]
•	For each bit → draw curved segment, ring, or Bézier
•	Use mapping functions for curvature, opacity, hue

→ Output static glyph or morph animation between states.

⸻

10. Philosophical Echo

Curves re-introduce the sense of flow and impermanence, aligning more closely with the I Ching’s original meaning as “Book of Change.” Straight lines symbolise order; curves symbolise transformation — together, they mirror how structure and flux coexist.

⸻

It includes the traditional hexagram, all six component waves, progressive partial sums (1→6), and the final composite curve — plus mapping and Processing-friendly pseudocode.

Direct image plates (PNG) • Traditional hexagram — Li: PNG • Component waves overlay (six harmonics): PNG • Partial sums (k=1..6): • k=1: PNG • k=2: PNG • k=3: PNG • k=4: PNG • k=5: PNG • k=6 (final sum): PNG • Final composite probability wave: PNG

What you’ll see inside the HTML • A clear mapping from hexagram lines (bottom→top) to harmonic components (n=1..6). • Rule: yang (1) → in-phase cosine; yin (0) → π phase shift (sign inversion). • Amplitudes decrease by 1/n to stabilise the shape (low harmonics dominate global structure; high harmonics add detail). • A light midline for reference; all strokes are black/white for print-friendly use.

Processing-friendly formula (also in the HTML) • y_i(x) = (1/i) * cos(2π * f0 * i * x + φ_i) with φ_i = 0 for yang, π for yin; f0 ≈ 0.7. • Composite: Y(x) = norm(Σ_i y_i(x)). • Count lines bottom→top to set i = 1..6 consistently.

Each curve can be described as a linear equation? What forms can this equation take?

Short answer: the curves aren’t “linear in x” (i.e., not straight lines), but they’re linear combinations of basis functions. That means you can write each curve in several equivalent linear forms—use whatever is most convenient for math or code.

Download — all forms in one tidy brief: I Ching — Superposition Curves: Linear Forms

The main forms (summary) 1. Amplitude–phase cosine sum (working form) Y(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i\cos(2\pi f_0 i x+\phi_i). Here w_i are weights (e.g., 1/i), f_0 is the base frequency, and \phi_i\in{0,\pi} encodes yin/yang. (Superposition idea.) Refs: Britannica (superposition); Wikipedia (Fourier series). 2. Cos–sin coefficient form (phase-free) Y(x)=\sum_i\big[C_i\cos(\omega_i x)+S_i\sin(\omega_i x)\big] with \omega_i=2\pi f_0 i, C_i=w_i\cos\phi_i, S_i=-w_i\sin\phi_i. For \phi_i\in{0,\pi}: S_i=0, C_i=\pm w_i. Refs: Wikipedia (Fourier series). 3. Complex exponential (phasor) Y(x)=\Re{\sum_i A_i,e^{j\omega_i x}}, A_i=w_i e^{j\phi_i}. Refs: Wikipedia (Euler’s formula). 4. Matrix / regression form (sampled points) y = X\beta with columns of X as \cos(\omega_i x_k),\sin(\omega_i x_k); \beta=[C_1,S_1,\dots,C_6,S_6]^T. Refs: Britannica (Fourier analysis). 5. Chebyshev / polynomial-in-\cos\theta Let \theta=2\pi f_0 x. Then \cos(i\theta)=T_i(\cos\theta) → Y(x)=\sum_i a_i,T_i(\cos\theta). Refs: Wikipedia (Chebyshev polynomials). 6. Frequency-domain (Fourier transform) distribution \hat Y(\omega) is a sum of delta spikes at \pm\omega_i with coefficients from (C_i,S_i). Refs: Wikipedia (Fourier series/transform), Britannica (Fourier analysis). 7. Linear differential equation satisfied by the curve Each component solves y’’+\omega_i^2y=0. The sum Y satisfies \prod_{i=1}^{6}(D^2+\omega_i^2)Y=0 (12th-order LODE for distinct \omega_i). Refs: Wikipedia (Linear differential equation; Harmonic oscillator).

Notes • The mapping is linear in parameters (w_i,\phi_i) or (C_i,S_i). • The optional normalization Y/\max|Y| (used in our graphics) is non-linear; omit it if you need strict linearity.

Citations • Fourier series / harmonics: Wikipedia — Fourier series; Britannica — Fourier analysis. • Euler’s formula / complex exponentials: Wikipedia — Euler’s formula. • Chebyshev polynomials: Wikipedia — Chebyshev polynomials. • Linear ODEs; harmonic oscillator: Wikipedia — Linear differential equation; Harmonic oscillator. • Superposition principle: Britannica — Principle of Superposition. • I Ching basics (context): Wikipedia — Hexagram (I Ching).

why are ines are counted from the bottom up (1→6). does it make any difference if they were coumted top down?

Short version: lines are counted bottom → top because that’s how you generate them during divination, and that’s how the classical commentaries reference them (“Nine at the beginning” = bottom line). Reversing the count would break consistency with both casting procedures and traditional line texts.

Why bottom → top? • Casting order. Both yarrow-stalk and coin methods produce the hexagram from the first (lowest) line upward to the sixth (uppermost), so “line 1” is naturally the bottom line.
• Classical usage. Standard translations label line texts “Nine/Six at the beginning” (i.e., at the bottom) and proceed upward, so scholarship and manuals assume this numbering.
• General convention. Reference articles explicitly state that hexagram lines are “traditionally counted from the bottom up.”

Would top → bottom make a difference?

Yes—in a few important ways: 1. Interpretation & moving lines If you renumber top-down but keep reading line texts in the usual order, you’ll apply the wrong line statements and produce the wrong resulting hexagram when lines change. (Traditionally, changes are applied to specific bottom-up line indices.)
2. Hexagram identity vs. its “reverse” Flipping a hexagram upside-down gives its reverse (contrasting) hexagram; many come in such inverse pairs (e.g., 43 ↔ 44). Changing the counting direction effectively treats the same picture as if it were its upside-down counterpart, which is a different hexagram in the literature.
3. Your waveform encoding In our project we map line position i=1..6 (bottom→top) to harmonic i with weight 1/i: Y(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{6}s_i,\frac{1}{i}\cos(2\pi f_0 i x), \quad s_i=\begin{cases}+1&\text{yang}\-1&\text{yin}\end{cases} If you renumber top-down, the sign sequence becomes s’i=s{7-i}. Because weights depend on i, the curve changes (it’s not just a re-label). Only if all weights were equal would the result be identical. (We can support both schemes if you ever want an alternate “top-down” export.)

Bottom line: Stick with bottom→top to stay interoperable with cast methods, line texts, lookup tables, and standard references. If you really need top→down for a particular visualization, we’ll remap the indices explicitly so your outputs stay internally consistent and clearly labeled.

how would meta-symbol work?

Love this idea. A meta-symbol is a single graphic that compresses the structure of the whole Yi into one “hexagram-like” mark, using the exact visual language we’ve been building (black/white static line drawings + superposition curve). Here are three robust ways to define it, all reproducible with your current pipeline, and all yielding a unique closed-form formula to print under the curve.

1) Sequence-axis (PCA) meta-symbol

What it captures: the dominant “direction” that runs through all 64 superposition curves in King Wen order—i.e., the shape that explains the largest shared variance across the set.

How to build 1. Sample each curve Y_k(x) (k = 1…64) on a dense grid x\in[0,1]. 2. Stack them into a 64×N matrix and compute the first principal component over x. 3. Project that component back into our 6-frequency cosine basis to get printable coefficients c_i: M_{\text{PCA}}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{6} c_i\cos(2\pi f_0 i x)\quad\text{(with our usual }f_0=0.7\text{).} Pros: derived from all hexagrams; order-aware (King Wen). Cons: needs a tiny linear-algebra step, but it’s fully deterministic and fast.

Why it’s legitimate: the Yi canon is 64 figures in King Wen order; treating the sequence as a dataset and extracting its main “axis” respects that classical arrangement.

2) Completion-dyad meta-symbol

What it captures: the tension between the final pair 63 (After Completion) and 64 (Before Completion)—traditionally read as a cyclic hinge. We define: M_{\text{dyad}}(x)=\operatorname{norm}!\big(Y_{63}(x)-Y_{64}(x)\big). Because 63 has bits 101010 (Li over Kan) and 64 has 010101 (Kan over Li), their signs are exact opposites at every line, so the difference yields a strong, alternating spectrum that’s visually striking and thematically apt for a “65th” meta.

Pros: conceptually crisp; closed form lands directly in our printed 6-term equation (the coefficients are just the alternating signs scaled by 1/i). Cons: based on the last pair only (but those two are the canonical finale of King Wen).

3) Nuclear-core meta-symbol

What it captures: the Yi’s inner “core” dynamics by weighting the middle lines (2–5) via nuclear hexagrams (lower nuclear = lines 2–4; upper nuclear = lines 3–5). Two ways:

(a) Nuclear average. Average Y_k(x) over the 16 nuclear results only: M_{\text{nuc}}(x)=\frac{1}{16}\sum_{h\in\mathcal{N}}Y_h(x), then fit to \sum_i c_i\cos(2\pi f_0 i x).

(b) Line-centric nuclear weighting. Keep all 64, but amplify contributions from lines 2–5 (the nuclear “window”) by a factor w_i (e.g., w_1{=}w_6{=}1,;w_{2..5}{=}2): M_{\text{nucW}}(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{64}\sum_{i=1}^{6}\frac{w_i}{i},s_i^{(k)}\cos(2\pi f_0 i x)\Bigg/\text{norm}. This foregrounds the Yi’s “inner” lines, aligning with how nuclears are constructed.

Pros: grounded in a classical structural idea (nuclear hexagrams). Cons: you must break the global yin/yang symmetry, otherwise a straight, unweighted all-64 mean cancels out at each line position (32 yang vs 32 yin). The nuclear focus/weights do exactly that.

why is the diagram symetrical?

Short answer: because for every hexagram curve Y(x) you plotted, its complement (swap every yin↔yang line) produces the curve -Y(x). When you overlay all 64, every shape appears together with its vertical mirror—so the composite looks perfectly symmetric about y=0.

Why that happens in your mapping • Your rule is linear: each line contributes \pm \frac{1}{i}\cos(2\pi f_0 i x) ( “+” for yang, “−” for yin ). Flipping all six lines just flips all six signs, so the whole sum is multiplied by -1: the complementary hexagram gives -Y(x). The King Wen corpus contains each hexagram and its complement, so both Y and -Y are in the stack. Overlay ⇒ vertical symmetry.
• Independently of pairings, across the full set of 64 there are exactly 32 yin and 32 yang at each line position. For any fixed x and harmonic i, half the curves add +\frac{1}{i}\cos(2\pi f_0 i x) and half add the negative. So the mean over all 64 is zero at every x, centering the “cloud” on the horizontal axis. (This is the same parity balance the King Wen pairing exploits.)
• The cosine basis you use is even (\cos(-\theta)=\cos\theta), which helps keep contributions balanced in sign once complements are included—though note it’s the complement pairing that guarantees the vertical mirror symmetry, not cosine’s evenness by itself.

So what symmetry should you expect? • Vertical (about y=0**): exact, because Y(x) and -Y(x) are both present (and normalization doesn’t change that since \max|Y|=\max|-Y|). • Left–right (in x)**: not guaranteed—your domain is x\in[0,1], not symmetric about 0—so any left–right look-alike is incidental. • Band “thickness” symmetry: yes; at each x, the distribution of values is balanced above/below zero due to the 32/32 split at every line.

Each curve’s overall stroke scale is set by the hexagram’s expected number of moving lines under yarrow, and the stroke varies along the curve by local energy E(x)=\sqrt{Y(x)^2+0.4,Y’(x)^2}. • All 64 curves (A3 @ 300 dpi): All64_Curves_MovingStatic_YARROW_X_LocalEnergy_BOLD_A3_300dpi_2025-10-21.png • One per complementary pair — 32 curves (A3 @ 300 dpi): OnePerPair_32_Curves_MovingStatic_YARROW_X_LocalEnergy_BOLD_A3_300dpi_2025-10-21.png • Brief explainer (HTML): MovingStatic_YARROW_LocalEnergy_Explainer_2025-10-21.html

What’s under the hood • Yarrow line odds: 6,7,8,9 occur with probabilities 1/16,,5/16,,7/16,,3/16. So \Pr(\text{moving}\mid\text{yin})=1/8, \Pr(\text{moving}\mid\text{yang})=3/8. For a hexagram with k_{\text{yang}} yang lines, \mathbb{E}[#\text{moving}] = (6-k_{\text{yang}})\tfrac{1}{8} + k_{\text{yang}}\tfrac{3}{8} = 0.75 + 0.25,k_{\text{yang}}. I map this from [0.75,2.25] into a bold per-curve factor and multiply the segment-wise energy thickness.
• Coins for comparison: coins give 6,7,8,9 as 1/8,,3/8,,3/8,,1/8. Aggregated yin/yang are 1/2 each and \Pr(\text{moving}\mid\text{yin})=\Pr(\text{moving}\mid\text{yang})=\tfrac14, so every hexagram has the same expected number of moving lines (1.5). That’s why coin-based moving/static doesn’t differentiate curves unless you adopt a “modified three-coins” that reproduces yarrow odds.

• Per-point stroke: Along the curve we modulate thickness by local energy E(x)=\sqrt{Y(x)^2+0.4,(Y’(x))^2}, normalized per curve. • Why symmetry appears: The complement of any hexagram (flip every line) maps to -Y(x); overlaying all 64 includes both Y and -Y, so the composite is vertically symmetric about y=0. (Line inversion/complement and bottom→top counting are standard in hexagram construction.)
